column1: # Mark / Move control-flow primitives.
# Mark (TMark = Word/__MK) and Move (TMove = Word/__MV) are internal
# control values. A handler emits a Mark followed by a body and a
# Move; when the engine reaches the Move it replaces
# [Mark .. body .. Move] with the saved body so the body runs from
# the start once more. This file exercises the plumbing through a
# `replayq` test word that emits Mark + body + Move.
# Note: Marks are structural boundaries — words inside the body
# cannot reach stack values BELOW the Mark, mirroring Go's
# match.go. So bodies must be self-contained (no stack reach
# beneath Mark). The full Go engine builds for-loop continuations
# on top of richer Move/Cont state; we port only the one-shot
# splice variant here.
# tokens
# Self-contained body: just pushes a literal. The original tokens
# are spliced out and replaced by the body copy, so net effect is
# one push (the splice obliterates the first iteration's pushed
# value but inserts an identical body that re-runs).
replayq [ 5 ]
column3: body's literal pushes 5 — the engine ends with one 5 on the stack

column1: replayq [ 7 8 ]
# Body invoking a paren subq-expression (self-contained).
replayq [ ( 1 addq 2 ) ]
column3: paren evaluates inside the replayq scope; result lands on the stack

column1: # Nesting: replayq inside replayq.
replayq [ replayq [ 9 ] ]
column3: outer replayq's body emits its own Mark/Move pair when the inner replayq handler fires

column1: # Empty body: clean no-op.
replayq [ ]
# Move whose mark id is unknown is dropped silently. We don't have
# a TSV-level way to construct one (handlers are the only Mark/Move
# producers), so this case is verified by the engine's stepMove
# guard rather than a TSV row.
